text: The video shows two metastatic lesions located in the left lobe of the liver.
A 10 mm working port is visible on the right side, equipped with a reducer for instrument access.

The surgeon examines the left lobe of the liver during diagnostic laparoscopy, observing puckered metastases on the surface of the left lobe without finding any other grossly visible metastases elsewhere.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical structures including the cross of the diaphragm and the caudate lobe, with the inferior vena cava (IVC) visible nearby.
The surgeon separates the peritoneal adhesions, using instruments to carefully detach the tissue layers.
The surgeon frees lateral adhesions on the left lobe of the liver, focusing on the delicate left triangular ligament at the lateralmost part.

The surgeon identifies the left triangular ligament and points out the anatomical landmarks including the caudate lobe, the inferior vena cava (IVC), the diaphragm's crura, and the stomach with a mild hiatal hernia.

The surgeon dissects the right triangular ligament and the central part of the falciform ligament while performing a left lateral hepatectomy. The inferior vena cava (IVC) and a portion of the suprahepatic cava are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the phrenic vein, noting the potential for significant bleeding if the phrenic vein is injured.

The surgeon is dissecting the left hepatic vein, with the inferior vena cava visible adjacent to the left instrument. The left hepatic vein is clearly identified, marking the area for the final transaction.
The surgeon identifies the line of excision on the liver parenchyma, marking the area for resection during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the segment three pedicle, highlighting its location and vascular structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies an artery and the portal vein of segment three, preparing to divide these structures. An instrument is ready for use, and a hemolock is requested for securing the division.
The surgeon identifies the segment two and segment three pedicles, along with the artery, in the liver. These structures are visually distinguished and prepared for surgical intervention.

The surgeon clips the artery, then proceeds to take the segment three pedicle followed by the segment two pedicle. The hepatic vein is taken with a stapler, and the liver parenchyma is divided using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon applies a Hem-o-lok clip to the left lateral lobe hepatic artery, which is clearly visible due to the patient's non-obese physique and the liver's healthy appearance, lacking excessive fat or fibrosis.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the hepatic pedicles separately using harmonic scalpel and clips. If difficulty arises, staplers will be used to secure the pedicles.
The surgeon performs parenchymal dissection on a relatively free part of the liver, using precise techniques to separate tissue layers.

The surgeon is performing a liver resection, dividing the liver parenchyma incrementally using a harmonic scalpel. The finger fracture method is employed to separate liver tissue without activating the harmonic's energy.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device to delineate a vessel, employing a technique similar to finger fracture to confirm the vessel's location.
The surgeon controls oozing from a vessel located in the depth of the surgical field, dividing surrounding tissue to access and visualize the area effectively.
The surgeon identifies a vein and prepares to apply a surgical instrument, possibly a clamp, at a setting of thirty-five.
The surgeon uses a Powered Echelon Flex Endovascular White Stapler to perform vascular stapling during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon is dissecting the liver parenchyma near the ligamentum venosum, revealing a tributary of the left hepatic vein.
The surgeon reaches the main proper left hepatic vein, preparing to use a surgical instrument, possibly a 35mm stapler, for further manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the left hepatic vein and prepares to secure it using a 35 vascular load. The parenchymal transection is complete, and the vein is encircled to prevent accidental slippage. The phrenic vein and other structures are spared, and the main portal pedicle is not encircled during the left lateral hepatectomy.

The surgeon completes the resection of the liver, using a powered echelon 35 cartridge designed for vascular hepatectomy. The specimen is removed with minimal blood loss, and the blood pressure is stable at 100. The surgical field is clear, indicating successful resection of both metastatic lesions. The team prepares to proceed with colonic mobilization.
